data.title: U.S. Government Struggles To Keep A Lid On 10-Year Treasury Yield And Mortgage Rates
data.description:
The “rate check” on Friday to put a floor under the yen was the latest step.

On around midday Friday came the latest step, a “rate check,” with which Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent attempted to put a floor under the yen that had plunged against the dollar and push back down long-term US Treasury yields that had surged, as he saw the turmoil in the Japanese bond market and the plunge of the yen bleeding over into the US.

The New York Fed, at the request of the Treasury Department and acting as a fiscal agent for the Treasury Department, asked its primary dealers what exchange rate they would get if the NY Fed started buying yen through them. This “rate check” was a signal that the US government is ready to intervene in the currency market to support the yen against the USD.

As soon as this happened around midday Friday, the USD began to plunge against the yen, and the yen jumped from 159.2 yen to the USD to 155.7 by Friday evening (hourly chart via Investing.com):

On Wednesday, Bessent had blamed the surging long-term US Treasury yields on the bond-market meltdown in Japan, during which the 30-year Japanese Government Bond yield spiked by 42 basis points in two days and drove it to 3.91%, the highest since the 30-year bond was introduced in 1999. The crucial 10-year JGB yield surged by 15 basis points in two days. And the yen re-plunged against the dollar. The trigger had been Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s call for increased government spending with simultaneous tax cuts.

The 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) had surged to 4.30% on Wednesday morning, up by 17 basis points in a week, even as the Trump administration is trying to get mortgage rates to come down. But mortgage rates track the 10-year Treasury yield, with a varying spread. And this surge of the 10-year yield caused mortgage rates to jump back to 6.20%, from 6.01% a few days earlier, according to the daily measure of 30-year fixed mortgage rates by Mortgage News Daily. And Bessent blamed that on the bond market meltdown in Japan.

“It’s very difficult to disaggregate the market reaction from what’s going on endogenously in Japan,” Bessent told Fox News at the time. And he said that he’d gotten in touch with Japanese officials, and said that he is “sure that they will begin saying the things that will calm the market down.”

With this two-step three-day jawboning — first on Wednesday when Bessent said he “got in touch” with Japanese authorities, and then on Friday, with the “rate check” — the 10-year yield dropped from 4.30% to 4.23% (hourly chart via Investing.com).

To help push mortgage rates down, the Government Sponsored Enterprises Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac started buying back some of the MBS they’d issued. This started in 2025. But on January 8, this was moved to the front pages when Trump directed Fannie and Freddie to buy back $200 billion in MBS, about the maximum they can buy back under current legal limitations.

But they don’t have the available cash to do that, they only have enough available cash to buy some MBS. They could also issue bonds and then use the cash proceeds to buy those MBS, but that would put further pressure on the bond market. So whatever.

This announcement was a masterful if temporary stroke of jawboning down mortgage rates, which plunged by 20 basis points combined on Friday, January 9, and Monday, January 12.

It didn’t last long, however. By Wednesday, January 21, mortgage rates were right back where they’d been on January 8. They’d just done a big U (daily chart via Mortgage News Daily).

Obviously, Bessent wouldn’t blame the surging long-term Treasury yields on the ballooning US deficit and the flood of new supply of bonds coming on the market that investors will have to absorb, and he wouldn’t blame it on inflation that accelerated further and that worries the bond market. Jawboning is a lot easier to do than to address those issues.

The bond market might not be happy for long with this jawboning. Inflation is a big issue for bond investors as bonds lose purchasing power due to inflation, and yield is supposed to compensate for the loss of this purchasing power, plus some. But long-term yields are too low to compensate investors for hotter inflation in the future.

And inflation keeps moving further away from the Fed’s target, amid government policies of prodigious deficit spending and Trump’s pressure on the Fed to cut short-term interest rates. They want to run the economy “hot,” meaning higher growth and more inflation, which provides fertile ground for inflation to bloom. Given this scenario, the bond market is still very sanguine, surprisingly sanguine, despite the recent ripples.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: IDEX Corporation: Value Creator Is Not Delivering
data.description:
In the fall of 2024, I concluded that IDEX Corporation (IEX) was a great business, yet it was commanding demanding valuations. While the positioning towards mission-critical industrial and technology solutions was to be applauded, the issue was that organic growth was rather sluggish.

At the time, valuations reflected quite high expectations, with the P/E ratio at a rather demanding level of 30 times earnings. The issue is that the sluggish performance has continued, but in the meantime, shares have lost 15% of their value.

This makes it so that shares have dramatically underperformed the market, while multiples have come down to 24 times earnings. This has improved appeal, if not for the issue that this still represents a premium valuation, while the operating performance remains uninspiring, to put it mildly.

Critical Processes

IDEX provides engineered products that are focused on mission-critical solutions, with a focus on higher growth and highly specialized markets. The company generates over $3 billion in sales from such solutions, with these revenues split across three segments. Both health & science technologies and fluid & metering solutions make up nearly 40% of sales, complemented by a smaller fire & safety/diversified segment.

The business is quite diversified, with half of the sales generated in the US, as the remaining revenues are split pretty evenly between Europe and the rest of the world. Aided by its 80/20 operating model, IDEX has seen long-term growth and execution. This includes flexibility and accountability to foster the performance of the business. The business employs some 9,000 workers who are active in over 100 countries, as the niche position and culture make margins surpass a quarter of sales.

Some of these niche segments include energy, life science, data centers, semiconductors, water, pharma, instruments, etc. Often, there are high-end technologies and products related to dispensing, metering, fluids, etc.

Shares have seen considerable gains, but then again, so have these markets, as shares traded around the $70 mark in 2015, to trade largely range-bound between $150 and $250 per share over the past five years. Shares are now trading in the middle of the range at $195 per share. The recent performance explains this stagnation. While revenues grew by a cumulative 70% to $3.4 billion, growth has been much more muted recently. Operating margins increased a few points over this period of time, as very minimal buybacks have been demonstrated.

Cautious Take

In the fall of 2024, the company was struggling. 2023 revenues were reported down 3% to $3.3 billion as adjusted earnings rose by a percent to $8.22 per share. Moreover, sales were considered largely flat for the year 2024, with adjusted earnings expected to be flat at $8.30 per share. In fact, this guidance was coming in under pressure as the year progressed.

Struggling to deliver on organic growth, the company announced a larger $1 billion deal to acquire Mott Corporation, a producer of sintered porous material structures. While tax benefits reduced the effective purchase price to $900 million, the price was demanding at 19 times EBITDA and 4.5 times sales. This deal meant that net debt jumped towards $1.4 billion, although in all fairness, leverage was seen at just 1.5 times.

The issue was that of a $235 stock, which, based on the original near $8 per share guidance, made those earnings multiples come in around 30 times. While the long-term track record was unparalleled, this came after 2023 sales were down and the 2024 performance was underwhelming, all of which made me cautious here.

Coming Down Further

Early in 2025, IDEX reported flattish 2024 sales at $3.3 billion as organic sales growth for the year was negative but turned positive in the fourth quarter. Full-year adjusted earnings of $7.89 per share were down 4%.

The company guided for 2025 organic sales to increase by 1-3%, with adjusted earnings expected to be up to $8.10-$8.45 per share, marking a modest improvement from the 2024 results, but all this was not too convincing.

After posting largely flattish organic sales in the first half of the year, third quarter organic sales were up 5%, with reported sales up 10% to $879 million. So far so good, but in between the soft first half of the year, full-year organic sales were now seen up just a percent. Adjusted earnings are seen between $7.86-$7.91 per share, much softer than the original 2025 guidance, which was nothing out of the ordinary already.

Net debt is reported at $1.3 billion, with net debt down modestly in between dividends, modest share buybacks, and acquisition efforts, but this is no issue with EBITDA seen just over $900 million each year.

What Now?

With the business performance being stagnant since the fall of 2024, shares have been coming under some pressure as the $235 stock price has come down to $195 per share. This, of course, should be seen in the context of a raging bull market.

With earnings stagnant at $8 per share, and with that I mean absolutely stagnant, a premium of 29 times earnings multiples has come down to 24 times, which still marks a premium, but much less of a premium. While this is certainly improving, the issue is that IDEX has seen absolutely dreadful performance recently, as shares are far from cheap here.

This is certainly the case, as the company has not issued a convincing 2026 outlook yet, as the company issued cautionary words about the performance in 2026. Given all this, my problem is that a great long-term track record has not demonstrated, nor has it been demonstrated for years, with the permanence being flat for quite some years now.

All this makes me very cautious here, as shares certainly deserve a premium and perhaps a bigger premium than the lower multiple here, but that is provided that strong operating momentum returns. Given all this, I am erring on the side of caution here. I see a few reasons for sustained and improved operating metrics or returns, but that is needed to justify this valuation and drive appeal here, all of which still make me cautious.

data.title: DFEN: The 3x Aerospace And Defense ETF To Know
data.description:
When talking about a leveraged solution to participate, with very short horizons, in the strong-momentum Aerospace & Defense market, I think of Direxion Daily Aerospace & Defense Bull 3X Shares Direxion Daily Aerospace ETF (DFEN). It positions itself as a precise daily leveraged replication alternative of the underlying and fairly liquid (average daily volume 3 million of 237.70K).

Intro and Definition

DFEN is a daily 3x leveraged long ETF with an AUM of $504.53 million offered by Direxion. This means that the fund sets a daily target +300% of the daily performance of the reference index, which in this case is the Dow Jones U.S. Select Aerospace & Defense Index.

Like other leveraged ETFs, the expense ratio is above average, at 0.95%, to which is added an average bid/ask spread of 0.23%. It is useless here to talk about turnover or tracking error (indirect costs), considering that the fund replicates on a daily basis and with leverage.

A distracting element worth reflecting on is the size of the yield, equal to 6.82% according to SA. Looking at it is distracting because the fund is designed with the objective of daily replication; it will not have much impact on the final performance for those who use the fund with intraday exposures, and the yield derives precisely from this mechanism.

What Does DFEN Do?

The ETF therefore replicates 3x the performance of an index concentrated in companies in the defense and military segment, civil aeronautics, and components. Going more into detail on the index, it is a Market-cap weighted structure based on market capitalization and free-float. The result is an exposure practically centered on Aerospace & Defense (~98.6%). In this sense, it can be viewed here on SA from the performance of the iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF (ITA), which uses it as a benchmark.

But beware, DFEN’s objective is different: we cannot expect that adding DFEN to a portfolio will result in a 3x performance of the index over the long term. This is because DFEN has a short-term objective and a daily rest. Therefore, more than a strategic portfolio allocation, it is suited to the role of the object of a short-term tactical operation, even a daily one. Using this ETF for longer stretches of time would result in volatility drag that dampens returns.

This happens through the use of OTC derivatives that exchange the performance of the index against a fixed rate and are settled daily. The daily reset aims to maintain a 3x target (in terms of returns and partly volatility). More on risks associated with leveraged ETFs, such as DFEN, can be found on FINRA's page and this SEC bulletin.

Who Is DFEN For?

In the prospectus itself, there is an explicit reference to the fact that this is a tool for sophisticated investors. The reason is the daily leverage, which amplifies exposure in a non-homogeneous way due to the volatility of the underlying.

Here is a practical example that can help explain why this is a problem:

  • Day 1: sector +2% → DFEN +6%.
  • Day 2: sector –2% → DFEN –6%.

Result? DFEN loses more value than the underlying. Why? Because the –6% is applied to a larger base, while the previous +6% is not “preserved” due to the daily rebalancing and compounding effects, eroding DFEN's value.

Therefore, this potentially makes it more suitable for those who intend to take intraday exposures and not for those who want to amplify returns 3x on a long-term basis relative to their exposure to the aerospace and defense segment. Likewise, it does not seem suitable for those seeking stable flows, or income or dividend strategies, despite the fact that today it appears to have a positive distribution.

How Is DFEN Built?

Every day, DFEN does this: it builds a partial exposure of real equities and then a dominant synthetic one. Through swaps, it multiplies the daily variation (again, NOT the level of the index). Then at the end of the day, it closes, recalculates, and resets. The base is naturally that of the reference index, which, as we have seen, has a total exposure to the Aerospace & Defense sector (98.62%).

It does so while maintaining a fairly concentrated distribution; not by chance, the top two alone, General Electric Company (GE) and RTX Corporation (RTX), colloquially known as Raytheon, are practically ≈36.6% of the total. Expanding to the top 10, concentration reaches 74%, which makes this segment particularly vertical on a few stocks.

Likewise, one must not forget that the Aerospace & Defense segment is a sub-segment of the industrials sector, which still maintains a fairly cyclical behavior. Considering that today the S&P Global index has a forward P/E of 47x, decidedly higher than that of industrials, equal to 25x, this sets off an alarm bell. Even more so when considering that 25x of industrials are today above 90% of the forward P/E levels of the last 10 years for this sector. If one were to rely solely on relative valuation logic, one could almost say that DFEN approaches, with 3x leverage, a market in a late-cycle phase. While this would change over, the relationship to cyclicality remains.

Risks

The prospectus helps us here, or rather, it comes right to our aid and states that the fund can be wiped out even in a single day, despite the index not losing all of its value. In an extreme sense, this is somewhat a consequence of what we have seen so far. Even more so if we consider the extremity of valuations in the industrial sector and in the subsegment represented by DFEN, the risk of violent intraday moves (to the downside), including gap downs, would generate significant erosion, difficult to recover in the short term on DFEN, and it would not be the first time this has happened historically.

The prospectus gives this example to understand how an increase in volatility could negatively impact performance: if the index does 0% over 1 year with an annualized volatility of 25%, the leveraged fund potentially returns ~17%. And absurdly, with a volatility of 100%, the estimated loss is ~95%, even if the index is at 0%.

Peer Comparison

The non-leveraged solutions deserve attention in this comparison: for completeness, I list iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF (ITA) and State Street® SPDR® S&P® Aerospace & Defense ETF (XAR).

DFEN, by making use of a daily 3x leverage through synthetic instruments with a daily reset, is not suitable to represent the reference sector over the long term. Instead, the non-leveraged solutions are more suitable for a long-duration sector exposure. At the same time, final returns benefit from a more efficient cost structure.

From the graphical comparison, a clear distinction emerges between the two strategies: over a ten-year horizon, DFEN fails to keep pace with its peers, while shortening the analysis to just one year radically changes the picture.

Precisely during the phase of maximum momentum in the defense and aerospace sector, DFEN in fact proves to be the most remunerative alternative, also effective for those who hold the exposure for multiple days.

The reason is precisely due to the strong momentum of the underlying: since there has been no “cyclicality,” the negative effect of the daily leverage reset has not had the chance to act, and therefore the leverage has amplified the result. ITA, in fact, has a momentum grade of A+ according to SA.

Pros & Cons

To conclude, I think DFEN has quite evident advantages and distinctive traits:

  1. Short-term amplified exposure: it offers 3x of the daily movements of the aerospace & defense sector.
  2. High reactivity to intraday events: it allows trading a macro view without selecting individual stocks.

Of course, it would be untruthful not to also list the disadvantages:

  1. Not suited for the long term: This is because it has daily leverage, and results are not cumulative.
  2. High sensitivity to volatility: In fact, volatility generates volatility drag and capital erosion.
  3. Tracking difficulty: The typical transparency of passive management funds is lacking.

This article answers three main questions about DFEN:

  1. How does DFEN relate to its underlying index?
  2. What risks accompany DFEN's use in a portfolio?
  3. Which investors is DFEN suitable for?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Merck Walks Away: What The Failed Acquisition Means For Revolution Medicines
data.description:
Thesis

Revolution Medicines, Inc. (RVMD) stock has had a pretty turbulent start to 2026 with speculation of a takeover from a couple of different big players. The most recent was Merck (MRK), who have now supposedly walked away from the table after they failed to come to a deal. The stock, as a result, has plummeted today by about 18%. However, Revolution shares are still up about 115% on the three-month chart, and the company has a very busy 2026 ahead in terms of clinical readouts. The most notable is the upcoming Phase 3 trial data surrounding daraxonrasib. Now, because of the potential of this candidate and the broad range of tumors it can supposedly target, I do see more future interest in terms of a takeover. The trouble is that it can be hard to place an accurate valuation on the pipeline and thus the company since the potential of daraxonrasib is not too clear yet, as I'll explain. Because of that, and the extremely overvalued valuation due to takeover speculation, I think it is best for investors to wait and see what the Phase 3 trial data brings before buying in at current levels.

Revolution Medicines 3Q25 Results

Just to touch quickly on the last earnings, Revolution back in 3Q25 showed us a pretty aggressive scale-up into late-stage development. The financial performance was clearly driven by expanding clinical operations and commercialization prep. For the quarter, the company reported a net loss of about $305.2 million, a nearly double figure that of the same time last year. Now, this was primarily due to a sharp rise in R&D spending, which we saw jump to $262.5 million, up quite a bit from $151.8 million in 3Q24. As you know, Revolution is funding multiple global Phase 3 trials for daraxonrasib and also making efforts to advance zoldonrasib and elironrasib programs. Aside from research costs going up, G&A expenses also more than doubled to about $52.8 million, which suggests an increased headcount and stock-based compensation, in addition to the early commercial infrastructure buildout.

Despite the rise in operational spend, Revolution still remained pretty strong financially. They held $1.93 billion in cash and marketable securities, which was also helped out by the first $250 million tranche from its Royalty Pharma deal. We also saw them gain access to an additional $1.75 billion in committed capital. So overall operationally, the quarter was marked by some good major pipeline momentum. Enrollment for the pivotal RASolute 302 PDAC trial was starting to settle down, and elsewhere, the new Phase 3 trials in RASolute 304 adjuvant PDAC and also the then-upcoming RASolute 303 first-line PDAC were initiated/prepared. Also, daraxonrasib gained some high-value regulatory designations, including Breakthrough Therapy, Orphan Drug, and a National Priority Voucher, which was good to see. I think this should strengthen its overall path toward a potential accelerated approval. Now, the stock saw a nice 4% jump on the day of the 3Q25 earnings report, and since then, we’ve seen the stock gain good momentum, now up almost 99% since the last earnings. There have been, of course, speculations of a takeover by multiple companies, the first being AbbVie (ABBV) and now Merck.

Another Speculative Deal Not to Be

So, the big news recently is that Merck has now stepped away from acquisition talks. It initially sent shares about 24% lower since the market had been pricing in a strategic premium for Revolution’s RAS-oncology assets, particularly daraxonrasib. We also have to keep in mind their lead RAS(ON) inhibitor is in late-stage trials for pancreatic and lung cancer. There was a big investor premium tied to forward revenue from that asset, so Merck’s interest was driven mainly by the opportunity to grab a possible first-in-class, broadly applicable RAS-targeted therapy. I think that would have complemented Merck’s oncology franchise pretty well, which is currently built around Keytruda.

One of the main interest areas for Revolution is that daraxonrasib is showing promise in those very hard-to-treat KRAS-driven tumors like PDAC and colorectal cancer, where treatment options remain limited. So with or without Merck, that interest and premium tied to it will still remain since daraxonrasib is on the right track, as I'll explain. Now, according to some prior reports, the discussions centered on a valuation of about $28 to $32 billion, which is a hefty sum that would have represented a big premium to Revolution’s pre-rumor market value. I think it would have likely delivered an immediate windfall for shareholders, in the sense that many had years of speculative upside tied into the stock, and it was close to leading into a near-term cash/stock payout. Now, for investors, such a deal would also have de-risked the overall story simply from monetizing Revolution’s pipeline before those pivotal trial readouts. I think it would have locked in value regardless of future clinical outcomes.

So, there’s obviously a gap here between what Revolution believes the platform and their late-stage data are worth and what Merck is willing to pay. Also, keep in mind the acquisition was being discussed ahead of some very definitive Phase 3 results. It was a tricky place to be in and has now left the company once again fully exposed to those upcoming clinical catalysts. There have also been some reports that talks could restart sometime in the near term or that even another bidder could emerge. And after seeing the interest Revolution has gotten in recent times, I don’t see that as too hard to imagine. With the current pipeline they hold and the mere fact that they are open to discussions, it means there will always be interest. However, I, personally, don’t see any more talks of an acquisition before we see the RASolute 302 Phase 3 trial readout, which is expected sometime this year. And the reality now is that the abrupt end just goes to show us how sensitive Revolution’s valuation now is. It’s sensitive both to M&A optionality and to the success of trial readouts. These readouts I now see carrying an even greater weight in shaping future strategic interest.

Daraxonrasib

It's clear that daraxonrasib/RMC-6236 has gained the most attention, being Revolution’s lead investigational targeted cancer therapy. Now, it's being designed to essentially inhibit RAS-driven signaling at its source, which is a mechanism that, historically, has been extremely difficult to drug. To clarify this, a lot of first-generation KRAS inhibitors bind only to very specific mutant forms of KRAS, and only when they’re in their inactive state. Whilst daraxonrasib, on the other hand, is a multi-selective inhibitor that preferentially binds the active, so GTP-bound, form of RAS across a pretty broad set of oncogenic mutations. And it’s specifically this range of mutations that has sparked such interest, with the list including KRAS G12X, G13X, Q61X, and potentially other RAS variants. Now, to do this, the molecule forms a tri-complex with RAS and a cellular chaperone such as cyclophilin A. This complex can actually block RAS from interacting with downstream effectors such as RAF and PI3K and, in theory, should suppress those proliferative signaling pathways central to tumor growth. It's a very interesting mechanism when you dig into the details, and what makes the candidate even more attractive is that it is an oral small molecule. As you know, oral therapies have attracted massive tailwinds in recent times purely because of their ease of use on the patient's end. They appear as much more attractive options over injectables or other administration methods.

Also, keep in mind that, mechanistically, the approach I described is pretty significant since it targets RAS when it is actually active. So that fact alone can overcome the limitations of other inhibitors that can only rely on rare mutation-specific pockets or those RAS “OFF” state moments. So the drug can offer a rather broader coverage across multiple RAS mutations in cancers. It includes the likes of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), and colorectal cancer. So, on that applicability, it can be hard to get a decent estimate of the TAM. I think this is another talking point that must have seen a fair bit of discussion in the negotiations. Revolution will inevitably think that their candidate can cover all these mutations effectively, whilst Merck, on the other hand, is probably pricing daraxonrasib as the worst-case scenario without yet seeing the results from the Phase 3 trials.

Just to touch on the current status in the pipeline, daraxonrasib is now advancing into late-stage clinical development. With the current challenge being Phase 3 registrational trials. Now, the most mature of these is, of course, RASolute 302. It’s a global, randomized Phase 3 study that looks to compare daraxonrasib monotherapy to the current standard-of-care chemotherapy in patients with previously treated metastatic PDAC. There are primary endpoints of progression-free survival and overall survival. Right now, the trial is winding down enrollment with an expected data readout sometime in 2026, as I mentioned. Also in parallel, daraxonrasib is being tested in RASolve 301, a Phase 3 trial in previously treated RAS-mutant NSCLC. There are also plans underway for earlier-line registrational trials in first-line PDAC coming in RASolute 303 and for adjuvant PDAC in RASolute 304. So it's a busy upcoming year for Revolution.

Specific TAM for KRAS-mutant Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma

As I said, one of the talking points in the takeover discussion had to be concerning an accurate estimate of the TAM for daraxonrasib. The way I see it, the most likely use of the drug, should we see an acceptance, would be specifically the treatment of KRAS-mutant pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC). Now, pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma is one of the most lethal cancers, and it makes up about 90% of all pancreatic cancer cases. On top of this, KRAS mutations themselves are present in roughly 90% of PDAC tumors, which should make the RAS-targeted therapy highly relevant as an option for the majority of patients with this disease. Now, in the U.S. alone, there are about 60,000 new PDAC diagnoses each year, and based on the KRAS prevalence data we have, that translates to roughly 50,000 to 55,000 new KRAS-mutant PDAC patients each year. On the global side of things, the incidence is correspondingly larger, given the similar KRAS mutation rates we see in Europe, Asia, and the other major oncology markets.

So when it comes to the global pancreatic cancer treatment market, which would, of course, encompass chemotherapy, targeted therapy, and then any supportive care, the current valuation sits at several billion dollars and is expected to grow pretty significantly over the next decade. Some estimates range from about $3 billion in 2024 to over $9 to 10 billion or more by 2030. However, because there are actually no broadly effective approved targeted therapies for the dominant KRAS mutations in PDAC, the potential of this drug can become a bit speculative, and it would almost be down to how much of a premium it could command if brought to market. And since most KRAS mutations like G12D, G12V, and G12R are not addressed by existing inhibitors like sotorasib and adagrasib, I see daraxonrasib having potentially a lot of demand. It would, of course, make it a first-in-class, broadly active KRAS(ON) inhibitor.

RVMD Stock Valuation: The Risk of Buying in Now

If you glance over Revolution’s valuation page, it doesn't screen too well. You’ll see the current valuation as a reflection of an early-revenue biotech, which is now being priced almost entirely on future pipeline expectations rather than its current financial performance. It means some metrics get elevated out of proportion, especially after the stock run-up on the back of the takeover speculation. Now, they have minimal commercial revenue and ongoing heavy losses, which means that the most relevant metrics, in my opinion, are price-to-sales and enterprise value. And in that sense, the stock also screens as extremely expensive. Revolution currently trades at roughly 25,000x trailing sales and over 6,000x forward sales, way above healthcare sector norms. It also highlights just how little revenue they have to support the now very hefty $22.7 billion market cap.

As of the last earnings, the company showed us a pretty strong cash position of about $1.93 billion with the addition of a $250 million royalty monetization tranche. So if we take their recent spending as an estimate for cash burn, the company’s operating losses have actually climbed rather sharply with all the major investments in late‑stage programs. They reported net losses of about $305 million in 3Q25 alone and a full‑year guidance that tipped at over $1 billion in GAAP net losses. So that would imply an annual cash consumption somewhere in the order of $800 million plus. At that current pace, that cash sum would fund operations into 2H27.

Elsewhere on a balance sheet basis, the stock trades at about 14x book value. So again, there’s a premium investors are assigning to its RAS-oncology pipeline, especially daraxonrasib’s potential. We have to be honest; the stock is trading on the expectation of successful Phase 3 readouts, with many believing a successful outcome has already been priced in. There’s also the potential of eventual blockbuster oncology sales, which can be hard to put a valuation on. Overall, this makes the shares highly sensitive to clinical news and M&A speculation, as you’ve seen. So at current levels, the valuation implies a substantial amount of that expected future success is already priced in, which makes me wary of initiating a buy rating on the stock. Also, I don’t think the speculation of a Merck takeover has been fully priced out yet. I think there’s a good chance they can come back to the table post-Phase 3 data. So I think it's best for investors to hold until the picture becomes a bit clearer.

Looking Ahead

For now, investors should be most focused on the upcoming Phase 3 readout from the RASolute 302 trial. This will be the clearest and most significant near-term clinical catalyst for daraxonrasib. Even without a takeover, we need this candidate to progress and gain more stock momentum. The other milestones I see would include the first-line PDAC data from the RASolute 303 trial and adjuvant PDAC data from RASolute 304. As for a financial update, Revolution is expected to report for 4Q25 sometime in late February 2026, which should give us an update on the timeline for a few of their candidates and also their latest cash position.

data.title: Likely 9.55% To 10.25% Returns On Two Harbors Preferred Stocks With UWM Buyout
data.description:
Introduction

At our Conservative Income Portfolio service, besides locating very undervalued preferred stocks and baby bonds, we look for special situations offering high returns. This article presents an example of the kind of special situation that we look for. So let’s dig in and look at the companies TWO and UWM in this example.

TWO Harbors (TWO)

TWO Harbors is an agency mortgage REIT (mREIT). Agency mREITs only buy government-guaranteed mortgages, which means they take on no credit risk when they purchase mortgages for investment. They make money by borrowing at lower short-term rates and using that money to buy mortgages that pay a higher rate of interest. So they make money on the spread between their cost of borrowing and the yield on the mortgages they buy.

TWO also invests in mortgage servicing rights (MSRs). TWO earns fees for servicing mortgages. The primary risk of MSRs is that interest rates fall and some homeowners refinance their mortgages that TWO is servicing, and TWO loses the fee for servicing those mortgages. However, currently the average interest rate on the mortgages TWO services is 3.96%. TWO bought a lot of their MSRs when rates were lower, and nobody who has this mortgage rate is going to be looking to refinance when current rates are over 5.25%. So MSRs look to be a relatively stable investment currently with low prepayment rates.

RoundPoint

A couple of years ago, TWO bought RoundPoint. Prior to the purchase of RoundPoint, TWO contracted out the mortgage servicing, which meant that they didn’t get to keep all the mortgage servicing fees but had to pay some of it to the contractor who actually did the servicing work. RoundPoint brought the servicing in-house so that TWO can keep the complete fees paid for servicing their mortgages for which they have servicing rights.

Having a direct line to the borrower now lets TWO know when the borrower is looking to refinance. Thus, TWO can offer to provide them with a new loan and capture the servicing right on the new loan. Thus, this lowers the amount of MSRs that they lose due to refinancing.

RoundPoint also has a platform to originate loans. TWO was primarily using that platform to originate new loans to those looking to refinance loans that they are currently servicing or to those who sold their home and bought a new home with needs of a new mortgage. RoundPoint allows TWO to directly market to the customers for which they are currently servicing.

TWO Common Stock

For those interested in the common stock of TWO, each share of TWO is set to receive 2.3328 shares of UWM upon the closing of the buyout. So your shares will trade similarly to UWM. With UWMC trading at $5.79, each TWO share is worth $13.50. TWO currently trades at $13.37, indicating there is little to be made in the arbitrage between TWO and UWM. This narrow spread also indicates the market’s strong conviction that this merger will happen. With TWO up 35% on the buyout, there seems to be no fear that the buyout won’t happen and TWO will drop back down in price.

Both companies's boards have approved the merger, and UWM voting is controlled by management, which certainly will vote yes on the merger. And with TWO common stock up by 35% as a result of the buyout, I can’t see TWO shareholders voting against the buyout as TWO traded at a discount to NAV before the buyout and now trades at a large premium to book value.

UWM Holdings

UWM Holdings is the largest mortgage provider (originator) in the USA. Don’t be fooled by their relatively low stock price. After the buyout, UWM will have a massive 1.87 billion shares outstanding. They are also the low-cost mortgage provider.

Like TWO, UWM also owns MSRs. What UWM really values in the buyout is TWO’s MSRs and getting their hands on RoundPoint. UWM plans to sell TWO’s mortgage holdings after the buyout. And after the buyout, UWM will have lowered leverage on their balance sheet, their book value will increase, and the company expects earnings to increase.

Getting their hands on RoundPoint is the gem for UWM. Currently, UWM also contracts out the actual servicing of their MSRs. RoundPoint would allow them to bring the servicing in-house saving them money and allowing them to have a direct relationship with all the homeowners that they are currently servicing. So like TWO, they can offer refinancing to anyone who is paying off their current mortgage by either refinancing it or moving to a new home and needing a new mortgage.

Additionally, UWM currently gets their mortgages through independent mortgage brokers who, when they find a homeowner who wants a mortgage, lock in the rate with UWM. UWM then securitizes (bundles up) these mortgages and sells them to Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, etc...and makes a profit off these securitizations. They don’t hold mortgages for investment, so they don’t have the kind of long-term interest rate sensitivity or risk that mREITs do.

Since UWM will now have a platform to originate mortgages themselves via RoundPoint, this should increase their profit margins and reduce reliance on independent brokers. UWM estimates that the synergies or savings from merging with TWO will be about $150 million dollars. From what I can tell from my research, this looks like a merger that makes a huge amount of sense for both TWO and UWM.

And UWM has also recently spent a fair amount on technology, which should improve their marketing ability. They have a new AI assistant loan officer, which has started generating a lot of business. Here is an article about that. And management says that if the 10-year note yield hits 4%, their business should pick up substantially.

Here is the stock chart from when UWM announced the merger until today.

UWM sold off after the merger announcement, maybe due to the knee-jerk reaction to the issuance of more shares to buy TWO, thinking about the dilution. But since the company has held an investor call and explained the benefits of this merger, the stock of UWM has streaked higher from around $4.40 to $5.79, or 31% higher. The company has a nice 6.9% dividend for a common stock, and if you believe that long-term rates are going lower, UWMC common could work well. But personally, I would wait for a pullback in the stock price before doing any buying of the common stock.

TWO Preferred Stocks Very Likely to Generate 9.55% to 10.25% Returns

The market sees UWM as a much safer company than TWO. Not long ago, TWO issued a bond (TWOD) with a 9.375% YTM at par. This bond matures in 2030. UWM has a 2030 bond, and the yield on their bond is a mere 5.80%. So UWM can get credit and raise money at a much lower yield than TWO can, and this low yield indicates the market’s strong confidence in the safety of UWM.

The TWO preferred stocks will become preferred stocks directly from UWM after the buyout and not from a subsidiary of UWM. Because of the high yields on TWO preferred stocks and UWM’s ability to raise cash at a much lower yield, they plan to call TWO-A, TWO-B, and TWO-C. This is stated in their investor presentation on the following slide showing pro forma capitalization after the merger.

The note at the bottom of the above image may be hard to read, but what it says is:

Note: Illustratively assumes monetization of TWO’s $6.5bn securities portfolio, including derivatives and repayment of $6.4bn of related financing; redemption of $115mm TWO senior notes outstanding; repayment of $261mm TWO’s convertible senior notes; and redemption of $622mm preferred shares with excess cash.

This makes total sense for them to do. TWO-C currently yields 9.42%. With UWM's ability to issue debt at 5.8%, it would be madness not to call TWO-C, which is callable right now. And on top of this huge spread between UWM’s cost of capital and TWO-C’s yield, UWM can write off the interest expense on their bonds while they cannot write off their preferred stock dividend payments. After tax, interest on their bonds costs them 4.1%, while TWO-C costs them 9.33% at par.

TWO-C (TWO.PR.C)

With the closing of the merger expected in the second quarter of this year, let’s assume TWO-C is called on June 30th, 2026. The yield to call (YTC) will be 10.26% at its current price of $24.84. This is what I call a “special situation” type of investment. That is quite a large return for a relatively short-term investment of—likely around 5 months.

And in the very unlikely situation that TWO.PR.C is not called after the merger; you will earn 9.42% until the merger is completed with a tax break that REITs get where 20% of your dividends will be tax-free. So the after-tax yield will be quite large. And once the merger closes, the dividend on TWO-C will become a “qualified dividend” with an even higher after-tax yield. For U.S. investors in the higher tax brackets, the after-tax yield on TWO-C will be equivalent to a 12.15% bond.

So we don’t even need the call to happen to make TWO-C a great investment. The increase in safety of UWM versus TWO plus TWO-C becoming a “qualified dividend payer” in itself makes TWO-C a great investment.

TWO-A (TWO.PR.A) and TWO-B (TWO.PR.B)

These preferred stocks cannot be called until next year—TWO-A in April and TWO-B in July. The YTC on TWO-A is 9.55% at a price of $24.55. While lower than that of TWO-C’s YTC, you will receive that high yield for a longer period of time, making it very attractive (assuming it is called as expected and as the company states). And TWO-B has a 9.67% YTC at a price of $24.27 for an additional 3 months beyond TWO-A. Additionally, once the merger closes, TWO-A and TWO-B will start receiving “qualified” dividends, nicely boosting their after-tax yield between the closing of the buyout and their call dates.

And if they are not called and they convert to floaters, their current yields will become higher yet if short-term interest rates don’t collapse between now and their call (floating rate) date. With the current SOFR, TWO-A is set to float at a 9.89% “qualified” yield while TWO-B is at a 9.22% “qualified” yield.

Summary

This special situation is a result of UWMC’s buyout of TWO. UWMC is primarily interested in getting its hands on TWO’s MSRs and their RoundPoint operation. UWMC expects to gain significant cost savings of $150 million from the merger. RoundPoint will allow UWMC to bring their mortgage servicing in-house and give them a platform to originate their own mortgages rather than purchasing them from independent brokers. The merger looks like a very good fit and is expected to close in the 2nd quarter.

Because UWMC is able to borrow money at low yields, they plan to call TWO’s 3 high-yielding preferred stocks—tickers TWO-A, TWO-B, and TWO-C.

TWO-C is callable now. With the merger expected to close in the 2nd quarter, if TWO-C is called on 6/30/2026, the yield-to-call (YTC) will be 10.26%. In the very unlikely circumstance that it is not called, TWO-C will become a preferred stock that pays a “qualified” dividend with a yield of 9.42% at its current price. With the dividend becoming “qualified” after the merger, the after-tax yield will be outstanding from a company that is able to issue debt at 5.8%, showing the confidence that investors have in UWMC’s safety.

TWO-A cannot be called until April of 2027 and has a yield-to-call of 9.55% and a floating rate of 9.89%. And all dividends post-merger will be “qualified.” TWO-B cannot be called until July of 2027 and has a YTC of 9.67%. And in the unlikely circumstance that UWMC decides not to call TWO-B, its floating rate will be a “qualified” yield of 9.22%.

data.title: Traders Defensively Positioned As Equities Retrace From Record Highs
data.description:
By Ed Tom

Cross-Asset Volatility: Implied volatilities were higher across the major asset classes last week as the markets strove to process the economic implications of the US’ demand for Greenland. Gold volatility led the advance as the safe haven asset shattered the $5,000/oz threshold and surged to record highs. GLD 1M implied volatility closed at 28.5 (3.5 std. dev. above its long-term average), levels last seen Oct 2025 as gold breached the $4,000 threshold and on par with levels triggered by the onset of the Russia invasion of Ukraine in 2022. GLD option volumes hit a record high last week as well, with over 2.3M contracts trading last Tues (almost 5x the YTD ADV). Option sentiment for the metal was overwhelmingly bullish with the call/put volume ratio at 4.2, more than double GLD’s typical call/put ratio of 1.8. Options activity in silver has exceeded even that of gold with SLV 1M implied volatilities hitting a high of 82.5 as silver continued its phenomenal breakout above $100/oz. Although SLV options volumes activity is at record highs, options sentiment is decidedly more bearish by comparison with SLV 25- delta put-call skew at a 94th percentile high. Heading into this week’s FOMC, the derivatives markets are pricing in a relatively benign meeting. Interest rate volatilities (VIXTLT) currently stand at 8th percentile lows, and Fed Funds futures currently imply a 97% probability that the Fed will keep rates unchanged.

Equity Volatility/Skew: Although the unexpectedness of last week’s geopolitical/trade tensions initially sparked fears of a Liberation Day-style market fallout, global equity volatility markets showed much more resilience this time around. Both the VIX® Index and the VSTOXX increased ~3-4 vol pts (roughly 20% of the Liberation Day volatility surge) before closing the week’s session only marginally higher w/w with the VIX Index at 16 and VSTOXX at 17.5. Vol of vol was likewise very controlled, with the VVIX Index increasing 15 pts to 117 (vs. +74 pts on Liberation Day). A large part of the reason why the equity volatility markets were so well controlled was due to the defensive positioning of traders heading into the event. As shown in Exhibit 2, S&P skew heading into Liberation Day was extremely flat (i.e., positioning of defensive puts was especially light). By contrast, S&P skew heading into last week’s Greenland announcement was (and still remains) at a 98th percentile high, indicating a high degree of downside hedging.

Correlation/Dispersion: Stock dispersion has steadily increased with the DSPX dispersion index rising 5pts to 34.8 ytd as META, MSFT, TSLA & AAPL gear up to report earnings this week. Despite geopolitical tensions, SPX implied correlation (COR1M) currently trades near an all-time low of 8.33.

Cross-Asset Volatility Monitor

Cross-Asset Volatility Snapshot (10Y Lookback)

How to read this chart:

The chart shows how various asset class implied volatilities are trading relative to their own history, using a 10-year lookback.

The volatilities are ranked by z-scores, which compares the current volatility level for an asset versus its own 10-year history. A z-score of +2, for example, would mean that the current volatility level is trading 2 standard deviations above its long-term average (i.e. rich) while a z-score of -2 would mean it’s trading 2 standard deviations below average (i.e. cheap)

Because each asset class volatility trades at different levels and often defined differently (e.g. lognormal vs. normal vol, price vs. bps vol), by standardizing using zscores, we’re able to compare different asset class volatilities on the same scale - and see at a glance if there are any divergences or dislocations in the cross asset volatility markets
